summary:
The Khoisan peoples of southern Africa produced an extensive and enduring tradition of rock art — paintings and engravings applied to cave walls and rock shelters — that extends back at least 25,000 years and continued into the historic period. During the era from 10,000 to 8,000 BCE, as postglacial climatic shifts transformed the southern African landscape, Khoisan communities were actively creating imagery on rock surfaces across what is now South Africa, Namibia, Botswana, Lesotho, and Zimbabwe. This tradition represents one of the longest-lived continuous artistic cultures known anywhere in the world.

Khoisan rock art is dominated by images of eland and other antelope, therianthropic figures (part-human, part-animal), and scenes of movement and transformation strongly associated with shamanistic practices and altered states of consciousness. Scholars working from ethnographic records of nineteenth-century Khoisan communities have established that the art was produced by shamans during or following trance states, with the imagery recording visionary experiences and spiritual power. The San concept of potency — a spiritual force accessed through dance and trance — is considered central to understanding the paintings' meaning.
